3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Zamora, G., Wong, J. H., Al-Shoaibi, A. A. A., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., He, J., Lavender, J. M., & Baker, F. C. (2025). Cyberbullying victimisation was associated with greater manic symptoms in early adolescence: A prospective cohort study. Acta Paediatrica, 114, 1904-1909.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly stated; data from period 2 follow-up (2018-2020) and period 3 follow-up (2019-2021)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We found that 2780 participants were missing data on cyberbullying, mental health symptoms, screen time and sociodemographics, resulting in a final sample size of 9095 adolescents aged 10-13 years for analysis (Appendix A).
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Manic symptoms also assessed at period 1 follow-up as covariate

3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Validated cyberbullying scale (Stewart et al., 2014) assessed at period 2 follow-up
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: We assessed the experience of lifetime cyberbullying by asking adolescents if they had ever experienced cyberbullying or if they had experienced someone intentionally trying to harm or be mean to them online, in texts, or on social media. We assessed the experience of past 12-month cyberbullying by asking adolescents if they had been cyberbullied in the past 12 months. Response options included yes or no.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is a covariate, not the primary exposure. Primary exposure is cyberbullying.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Manic symptom sum score at period 3 follow-up
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: 7 Up mania subscale from the 7 Up 7 Down Inventory
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: In our study, we used the 7-item 7 Up mania subscale, which was administered during the periods 1 and 3 follow-ups. The questions asked the adolescents about prolonged periods of extreme happiness and intense energy that interfered with sleep or affected the perceived vividness of their senses... All the items were summed to create a manic symptom sum score with values ranging from 0 to 21.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multivariable linear regression with propensity weights
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race and ethnicity, household income, parent education, anxiety, depression, total screen time, study site, manic symptom sum score at period 1 follow-up
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: One multivariable linear regression model was conducted to estimate the association between lifetime cyberbullying assessed at the period 2 follow-up and manic symptom sum score assessed 1 year later at the period 3 follow-up. A second multivariable linear regression model was conducted to estimate the association between past 12-month cyberbullying at the period 2 follow-up and manic symptom sum score at the period 3 follow-up. We report results for unadjusted models, as well as models adjusted for age, sex, race and ethnicity, household income, parental education, prior manic symptom sum score, anxiety, depression, total screen time and study site. Marginal predicted probabilities were computed following prospective linear regressions to get standardised estimates... Sampling weights were utilised in the analysis to represent population estimates and were based on the American Community Survey. Statistical significance was based on a two-sided p < 0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Manic symptom sum score at period 3 follow-up
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the presence of lifetime cyberbullying, versus the absence, was associated with a 0.41 higher (95% CI 0.18-0.65, p = 0.001) manic symptom sum score 1 year later

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Manic symptom sum score at period 3 follow-up
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the presence of past 12-month cyberbullying, versus the absence, was associated with a 0.38 higher (95% CI 0.11-0.66, p = 0.007) manic symptom sum score 1 year later

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Manic symptom sum score at period 3 follow-up
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Lifetime cyberbullying unadjusted B (95% CI) = 0.82 (0.57, 1.08), p < 0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Manic symptom sum score at period 3 follow-up
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Past 12-month cyberbullying unadjusted B (95% CI) = 0.75 (0.44, 1.05), p < 0.001

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Adjusting for the covariates, lifetime cyberbullying victimisation was associated with a 0.41 higher manic symptom sum score (95% CI 0.18-0.65, p = 0.001)
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The effect of 0.41 points on a 0-21 scale is quite small (approximately 2% of the scale range). The paper does not discuss the magnitude of this effect or acknowledge that on a 21-point scale this represents a trivial difference. The large sample (N=9095) provides power to detect very small effects.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: These findings highlight the need for early identification and intervention for adolescents experiencing cyberbullying to mitigate its adverse effects on mental health. Mental health professionals, educators and caregivers should be aware of the potential for cyberbullying to contribute to a broad spectrum of psychological disturbances, including manic symptoms.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion interprets the findings through the lens of etiological theories of bipolar-spectrum disorders, arguing that cyberbullying contributes to manic symptoms via stress sensitivity and circadian disruption. The paper recommends early identification and intervention without contextualizing that the effect (0.41 points on a 21-point scale) is very small. It frames the finding as supporting public health interventions and comprehensive prevention programs.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is used as a covariate in this paper, not as the primary exposure. The primary exposure-outcome relationship is cyberbullying to manic symptoms. This paper may not fit the review's focus on screen time as exposure.
